children.name: Gadus
children.description: Atlantic cod and its Pacific relatives — fish that shaped human history more profoundly than any other marine species. The collapse of the North Atlantic cod fishery is one of the defining ecological disasters of the 20th century: what was described as an 'inexhaustible' resource by John Cabot in 1497 was reduced to less than 1% of its 1950s population by 1992, when Canada imposed a total moratorium. The Baltic cod (torsk) is in similar crisis — it is currently in two populations, the eastern and western Baltic stock, and the eastern stock has collapsed due to a combination of overfishing, eutrophication (low oxygen in deep water), warming, and reduced salinity following changing precipitation patterns. Cod is still Sweden's most important marine food fish culturally, with lutefisk (lye-treated dried cod) being a traditional Christmas dish.

children.name: Gadus morhua
children.description: The fish that drove European colonisation of North America — the Grand Banks cod were the reason for Cabot's voyage. Baltic cod (torsk) is genetically distinct from North Sea cod. Eastern Baltic stock critically overfished; EU fishing quotas reduced to near-zero. Its recovery is one of European marine conservation's greatest challenges.

children.name: Gadus chalcogrammus
children.description: The world's largest single-species food fishery by volume — over 3 million tonnes caught annually in the Bering Sea. The fish in McDonald's Filet-O-Fish and most supermarket fish fingers. Arguably the most important fish in the global food system.

children.name: Gadus macrocephalus
children.description: A demersal cod of the North Pacific from the Yellow Sea to California, supporting one of the world's largest groundfish fisheries. Shorter-lived and faster-growing than Atlantic cod, with a prominent chin barbel.

children.name: Gadus ogac
children.description: An inshore cod of Arctic and subarctic coasts of Canada and Greenland, now often treated as a population of Pacific cod. Tolerant of cold, brackish coastal waters.

children.name: Melanogrammus
children.description: Haddock — a close relative of cod, distinguished by a black lateral line and a dark 'thumbprint' above the pectoral fin (according to legend, the mark left by Saint Peter when he plucked a coin from its mouth). Haddock prefers slightly deeper, colder water than cod and is found across the North Sea, Norwegian Sea, and into the North Atlantic. It is a superb food fish with white, flaky flesh — smoked haddock ('Finnan haddie') and haddock in traditional fish and chips are beloved British preparations. Unlike cod, the haddock population has shown genuine recovery in North Sea waters following improved management.

children.name: Melanogrammus aeglefinus
children.description: North Sea haddock populations have recovered well under sustainable fishing quotas. Feeds primarily on echinoderms, polychaetes, and bivalves on the seabed. More cold-tolerant than cod. Smoked haddock is a staple of British and Scandinavian cuisine.

children.name: Merlangius
children.description: Whiting — a slender, swift-swimming gadid of shallow coastal waters and the continental shelf from Iceland to the Mediterranean. Whiting is an important predator of small fish and invertebrates in the North Sea ecosystem and an important food source for seabirds, seals, and larger fish. It is the most cold-tolerant gadid, entering extremely shallow, inshore waters in winter that cod and haddock avoid. In Sweden and the broader North Sea, whiting are caught as bycatch rather than targeted species. Their extremely soft flesh deteriorates quickly, making fresh whiting challenging to market — much is processed into fishmeal.

children.name: Merlangius merlangus
children.description: Extremely abundant in the North Sea and Skagerrak, which form Sweden's western maritime border. An important food source for seabirds and larger fish. Soft flesh decomposes rapidly — must be consumed very fresh. Often bycatch in North Sea trawl fisheries.

children.name: Pollachius
children.description: The pollocks and saithe — pelagic schooling gadids of the North Atlantic, important food and forage fish.

children.name: Pollachius virens
children.description: Also called coalfish or coley, a fast-swimming North Atlantic pollock forming large schools. A major commercial fishery and increasingly marketed as a sustainable cod substitute.

children.name: Pollachius pollachius
children.description: The lythe or pollack of the northeast Atlantic, a popular sport fish around rocky reefs and wrecks. Distinguished from saithe by its protruding lower jaw and lack of a chin barbel.

children.name: Raniceps
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Tadpole Fish, an unusual deep-bodied gadid of European rocky coasts named for its frog-like head.

children.name: Raniceps raninus
children.description: The Tadpole Fish is a solitary, bottom-dwelling gadid found along rocky coasts and among kelp and seaweed beds from Norway and the British Isles south to the Bay of Biscay, typically at depths of 5–100 m. Its common name derives from its striking body shape — a disproportionately large, flat head tapering abruptly into a narrow tail — which superficially resembles a frog tadpole. It feeds nocturnally on small fish, crustaceans, and polychaetes and is rarely seen despite its wide range due to its cryptic, solitary behaviour in dense weeds and crevices.

children.name: Micromesistius
children.description: Blue whitings — small pelagic gadids of the Atlantic and southern Ocean fished in enormous quantities mainly for fishmeal and oil.

children.name: Micromesistius poutassou
children.description: A small, abundant mesopelagic gadid of the northeast Atlantic and Mediterranean, one of the most heavily fished species in European waters, mostly reduced to fishmeal.

children.name: Micromesistius australis
children.description: The southern counterpart of the Blue Whiting, occurring in large pelagic schools in subantarctic waters around South Georgia, the Falkland Islands, southern Chile, and New Zealand's subantarctic islands. It is heavily fished by trawls targeting the dense midwater aggregations that form during its spawning season and is a key prey species for rockhopper penguins, fur seals, and albatrosses.

children.name: Arctogadus
children.description: Arctic cods — a genus of two gadids inhabiting the Arctic Ocean and adjacent polar seas, adapted to cold near-freezing waters and often associated with sea ice.

children.name: Arctogadus glacialis
children.description: A circumpolar gadid of the high Arctic Ocean living at depths of 0–900 m, closely associated with the underside of sea ice and pack-ice margins where it feeds on amphipods, copepods, and small fish. Unlike the similarly named Polar Cod (Boreogadus saida), Arctic Cod tends to be larger and more demersal in habit. It is a crucial prey item for ringed seals, beluga whales, narwhals, and thick-billed murres, making it a keystone species in the fragile Arctic marine ecosystem increasingly threatened by warming waters.

children.name: Boreogadus
children.description: The polar cod — a keystone forage fish of Arctic ecosystems with antifreeze proteins in its blood.

children.name: Boreogadus saida
children.description: A small Arctic cod that lives among sea ice, producing antifreeze glycoproteins to survive sub-zero water. A critical link transferring energy from plankton to seals, whales and seabirds; vulnerable to sea-ice loss.

children.name: Trisopterus
children.description: Small short-lived gadids of European shelf seas — poor cod, pouting and Norway pout; three recognised species.

children.name: Trisopterus luscus
children.description: Also called bib, a coppery, deep-bodied gadid of northeast Atlantic reefs and wrecks with a long chin barbel and dark blotch at the pectoral base.

children.name: Trisopterus esmarkii
children.description: A small, abundant North Sea gadid forming the basis of a large industrial fishery for fishmeal and a key prey of cod, saithe and haddock.

children.name: Trisopterus minutus
children.description: A small, coppery-flanked gadid abundant across the northeastern Atlantic from Norway and the British Isles to the Mediterranean and Black Sea, typically found on muddy or sandy bottoms in 30–300 m of water. Poor cod rarely exceeds 25 cm and is short-lived, but it plays an important ecological role as prey for larger gadids, anglerfish, and seabirds. It is occasionally taken for human consumption, though its small size means it is more often used for fishmeal.

children.name: Molva
children.description: The lings — elongate, eel-like gadids with two dorsal fins found across the North Atlantic, prized as food fish; two species are recognised.

children.name: Molva molva
children.description: A large, slender gadid of deep rocky grounds in the northeast Atlantic, reaching over 2 m. An important commercial species sold fresh and salted.

children.name: Molva dypterygia
children.description: A deep-water relative of the Common Ling found along the continental slopes of the northeastern Atlantic from Iceland and Norway to the Bay of Biscay, typically at depths of 200–1,000 m over hard substrates. Blue ling is a slow-growing, long-lived species highly vulnerable to overfishing; it is commercially important in Norway and Iceland where it is dried and salted. The species congregates to spawn on rocky grounds, making aggregated populations easy targets for deep-sea trawlers.

children.name: Brosme
children.description: The tusk or cusk — a deep-water gadid with a single long dorsal fin.

children.name: Brosme brosme
children.description: Also called cusk, a solitary deep-water gadid of cold North Atlantic rocky bottoms, with a continuous single dorsal fin. Slow-growing and assessed as Near Threatened in places due to overfishing.

children.name: Eleginus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Saffron Cod, a small coastal gadid of cold subarctic Pacific and Arctic waters.

children.name: Eleginus gracilis
children.description: The Saffron Cod is a small, yellowish-olive gadid of shallow, cold coastal waters around Alaska, the Aleutians, and the western North Pacific from Japan to the Sea of Okhotsk, often entering estuaries and river mouths. It is an important subsistence food fish for Indigenous peoples of Alaska and Siberia, taken through ice fishing in winter. Spawning occurs under sea ice in early spring, and juveniles frequently use inshore kelp beds as nursery habitat.

children.name: Gadiculus
children.description: The silvery pouts — tiny deep-water gadids of the northeast Atlantic with large eyes and fragile silvery scales; two species are recognised.

children.name: Gadiculus argenteus
children.description: A small, big-eyed deep-shelf gadid of the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean with delicate silvery scales, important prey for larger demersal fish.

children.name: Gadiculus thori
children.description: A small deep-water gadid of the northeastern Atlantic and western Mediterranean, slightly larger than Gadiculus argenteus and distinguished by a rounder snout and more slender body. Like its congener it forms midwater aggregations on the outer continental shelf and upper slope, where it is an important prey item for larger commercial fish. Named after the Danish research vessel Thor, which collected the type specimens.
